subtopic_detail: High-dose folate after previous neural tube defect
stem: You are the GP seeing a 28-year-old woman planning a second pregnancy. Her first child, born 3 years ago, had a myelomeningocele. She has no diabetes, no epilepsy and takes no medications. She has a normal BMI and her diet is generally adequate. She has no known coeliac disease. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Examination: Unremarkable; cardiovascular and abdominal examination normal
data_table.Investigations: Serum B12 and ferritin normal; coeliac serology negative; HbA1c 5.0 percent

lead_in: What folate dose should be recommended for this preconception consultation?

options.text: Folic acid 0.5 mg daily starting now and continuing through the first trimester
options.rationale: 0.5 mg is the standard prevention dose for women without a personal or family history of neural tube defect; a history of an affected pregnancy needs the high-dose regimen. (Source: RANZCOG Preconception Folate)

options.text: Folic acid 0.8 mg daily commencing on confirmation of pregnancy through the first trimester
options.rationale: 0.8 mg is below the recommended high-dose level, and starting only after pregnancy confirmation misses the critical neural tube closure window at 28 days post-conception. (Source: RANZCOG Preconception Folate)

options.text: Folic acid 5 mg daily for at least 1 month preconception and through the first trimester
options.rationale: Women with a previous neural tube defect pregnancy need 5 mg folic acid for at least 1 month before conception and continuing through the first 12 weeks; this reduces recurrence by around 70 percent. (Source: RANZCOG Preconception Folate)

options.text: Folic acid 5 mg daily from the second trimester only with no preconception dosing
options.rationale: Folate cover is required before conception and through neural tube closure at around 28 days; starting in the second trimester does not prevent neural tube defects. (Source: RANZCOG Preconception Folate)

options.text: No folate supplementation if she eats a balanced diet that includes leafy green vegetables
options.rationale: Dietary intake alone is insufficient even with mandatory bread fortification; supplementation is essential for this high-risk group. (Source: RANZCOG Preconception Folate)

explanation.summary: High-dose periconceptional folate prevents recurrence of neural tube defects after an affected pregnancy. RANZCOG and Australian Pregnancy Care Guidelines recommend folic acid 5 mg daily for at least 1 month before conception and through the first 12 weeks for women with a previous affected pregnancy, diabetes, epilepsy on enzyme-inducing antiepileptics, malabsorption or BMI over 30. The standard 0.5 mg dose applies to women without these risks. Mandatory bread fortification supplements but does not replace supplementation.
key_points: Previous NTD pregnancy mandates 5 mg folate.
Start at least 1 month before conception.
Continue through the first 12 weeks of pregnancy.
Diabetes, epilepsy on enzyme inducers and BMI over 30 also need 5 mg.
explanation.pearls: Pre-conception iodine 150 micrograms daily is recommended alongside folate in pregnancy planning in Australia; routine multivitamin pregnancy preparations contain both but check the iodine dose because some pregnancy multivitamins still under-dose iodine relative to the NHMRC recommendation.

subtopic_detail: Cervical screening result management with HPV other genotypes
stem: You are the GP discussing a routine cervical screening test result with a 33-year-old woman in clinic. The laboratory reports oncogenic HPV (not types 16 or 18) detected, and reflex liquid-based cytology shows low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion. She is asymptomatic, has never had abnormal screening and is not pregnant. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Examination: Speculum examination on the day of sample was unremarkable; no contact bleeding; normal external genitalia
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening test: HPV (not 16/18) positive; reflex LBC: low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management step?

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in 12 months with referral to colposcopy if persistent
options.rationale: HPV (not 16/18) with negative or low-grade cytology is managed by repeat cervical screening test in 12 months; colposcopy follows only if HPV persists. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: Refer directly to colposcopy now given any positive HPV result and abnormal cytology
options.rationale: Australian guidance reserves immediate colposcopy for HPV 16/18 results or high-grade cytology, not for HPV (not 16/18) with low-grade cytology. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: Proceed to cervical cone biopsy under general anaesthesia at the next available list
options.rationale: Cone biopsy is a treatment step after histological diagnosis of high-grade disease; it is not part of routine triage in this scenario. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange punch biopsy of the cervix in clinic without prior colposcopic assessment first
options.rationale: Cervical biopsy is undertaken under colposcopic vision after appropriate triage; blind biopsy is not part of the screening algorithm. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

options.text: Return to routine 5-year recall because non-16/18 HPV carries minimal cancer risk overall
options.rationale: Non-16/18 HPV with abnormal cytology requires earlier follow-up than 5-yearly recall to detect persistent infection and progression. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program Guidelines)

explanation.summary: The Australian National Cervical Screening Program uses primary HPV testing with reflex liquid-based cytology. HPV 16 or 18 detection at any cytology result triages directly to colposcopy. HPV other types with negative or low-grade cytology triages to repeat HPV testing in 12 months. HPV other types with high-grade cytology triages to colposcopy. Persistent HPV at 12 or 24 months progresses to colposcopy regardless of cytology grade.
key_points: HPV 16/18 result triages straight to colposcopy.
HPV other plus LSIL triages to repeat in 12 months.
HPV other plus HSIL triages to colposcopy.
Persistent HPV at 12-24 months triggers colposcopy.
explanation.pearls: Self-collection of vaginal HPV samples is now equivalent to clinician-collected samples in the Australian program; offer self-collection to under-screened women including those who have experienced sexual violence or have cultural concerns about pelvic examination.

subtopic_detail: Gestational hypertension treatment threshold and target
stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a G2P1 woman at 32+0 weeks. Her booking BP was 110/70 with normal early bloods. Today routine review documents BP 156/98 on two readings 4 hours apart with no proteinuria on dipstick or PCR, no symptoms, no headache, no visual disturbance and no epigastric pain. Fetal growth and Dopplers were normal at 28 weeks. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, fundal height appropriate, fetal heart rate 144 with reassuring CTG, no peripheral oedema, no clonus
data_table.Investigations: Urine PCR negative; FBC, LFT, urate and creatinine normal; uric acid normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate antihypertensive plan?

options.text: Methyldopa 250 mg three times daily with a target BP below 120/80 in clinic
options.rationale: Methyldopa is acceptable but the target is set too tight; SOMANZ advises diastolic 80-85, not below 80, to avoid placental hypoperfusion. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Sublingual short-acting nifedipine 10 mg now to reduce blood pressure within minutes
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine causes rapid, uncontrolled drops in BP that risk placental compromise and is not part of Australian obstetric practice. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Admit for intravenous magnesium sulfate loading to prevent seizures and deliver tonight
options.rationale: Magnesium is reserved for severe pre-eclampsia or eclampsia; there are no proteinuria or end-organ features here and delivery is not yet indicated. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Plan immediate induction of labour at 32 weeks irrespective of corticosteroid timing
options.rationale: Delivery at 32 weeks is not indicated for gestational hypertension alone; expectant management with surveillance continues unless severe features or maternal-fetal compromise develop. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence oral labetalol 100 mg three times daily aiming for diastolic 80-85 mmHg
options.rationale: Sustained BP at or above 140/90 in pregnancy warrants treatment; labetalol is a first-line oral agent in Australia with a target diastolic BP of 80-85 from the CHIPS trial. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: SOMANZ guidance treats sustained BP at or above 140/90 in pregnancy with first-line oral labetalol, methyldopa, oxprenolol or nifedipine. Target diastolic BP is 80-85 mmHg following the CHIPS trial, balancing maternal stroke risk against placental perfusion. Severe hypertension (at or above 160/110) is a hypertensive emergency requiring rapid lowering with oral nifedipine, intravenous labetalol or intravenous hydralazine. Delivery is reserved for severe pre-eclampsia, eclampsia, fetal compromise or term gestation.
key_points: Treat sustained BP at or above 140/90.
Target diastolic 80-85 mmHg per CHIPS.
Labetalol is a first-line oral option.
Severe BP at or above 160/110 needs urgent acute therapy.
explanation.pearls: Beware silent renal impairment in gestational hypertension; a normal creatinine for a non-pregnant woman is a relatively high creatinine for pregnancy, and rising serum urate trends are an early biochemical marker of progression to pre-eclampsia.

subtopic_detail: Twin chorionicity at first trimester ultrasound
stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing the 11+4 week dating scan of a 30-year-old G1P0. The scan reports a twin pregnancy with two separate yolk sacs, a single placental site, two distinct amniotic cavities, a thin inter-twin membrane and a T-sign at the placental junction. Both fetuses have reassuring activity and nuchal translucency within normal range. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen with uterus appropriate for twin gestation; no bleeding; cervix long and closed
data_table.Investigations: Dating scan: twin pregnancy, single placental mass, thin inter-twin membrane, T-sign, two amniotic sacs, two yolk sacs, equal growth

lead_in: What surveillance plan should follow from these ultrasound findings?

options.text: Dichorionic diamniotic twin care with growth scans every 4 weeks from 24 weeks
options.rationale: A T-sign and single placental mass indicate monochorionic disease; dichorionic care is inadequate and risks missed twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Monochorionic diamniotic twin care with fortnightly ultrasound from 16 weeks for TTTS
options.rationale: T-sign with single placenta and dual amniotic cavities indicates monochorionic diamniotic twins; fortnightly scans from 16 weeks screen for twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome and selective growth restriction. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Monochorionic monoamniotic care with admission and continuous CTG from 24 weeks
options.rationale: Monoamniotic twins share a single amniotic cavity and lack an inter-twin membrane; this scan documents two amniotic sacs and therefore diamniotic disease. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Perform chorionic villus sampling now to determine zygosity before further surveillance
options.rationale: Zygosity is not required for surveillance; chorionicity drives surveillance and is already established by ultrasound features. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Defer assessment of chorionicity until the morphology scan at 18-22 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Chorionicity is most accurately determined in the first trimester; later assessment risks misclassification with potentially catastrophic surveillance consequences. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Chorionicity drives risk and surveillance in twin pregnancies and is best established in the first trimester. The lambda or twin-peak sign indicates dichorionic diamniotic twins. The T-sign indicates monochorionic diamniotic twins, who share a placenta but have separate amniotic cavities. Monochorionic monoamniotic twins share both placenta and amniotic cavity and have no inter-twin membrane. RANZCOG recommends fortnightly ultrasound surveillance from 16 weeks for monochorionic diamniotic pregnancies to detect twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome and selective growth restriction early.
key_points: T-sign and single placenta indicate MCDA twins.
Lambda sign indicates DCDA twins.
MCDA needs fortnightly scans from 16 weeks.
Determine chorionicity in the first trimester for accuracy.
explanation.pearls: In late-presenting twins where chorionicity cannot be confidently determined, default to monochorionic surveillance; the cost of over-surveillance is far less than the cost of missing twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome.

subtopic_detail: Hyperemesis gravidarum admission and refeeding precautions
stem: You are the GP reviewing an 8-week pregnant 26-year-old woman with severe nausea and vomiting. She has been unable to keep fluids down for 24 hours and has lost 4.5 kg from a baseline of 60 kg (a 7.5 percent loss). She feels dizzy on standing and has not voided urine since this morning. She has no abdominal pain, no fever, no haematemesis and no prior bariatric surgery. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 112, BP 100/65 lying with 22 mmHg systolic postural drop, T 36.7 °C, SpO2 98% room air
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, soft non-tender abdomen, no peritonism, no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Urine ketones 3+; sodium 132 mmol/L, potassium 3.0 mmol/L, urea 8.4 mmol/L; LFTs and TFTs pending; quantitative beta-hCG appropriate for gestation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Oral metoclopramide 10 mg three times daily with review again in 48 hours by phone
options.rationale: Oral agents are unreliable when the patient cannot retain fluids and electrolyte derangement is established; she requires parenteral therapy. (Source: RANZCOG Hyperemesis Gravidarum)

options.text: Trial of ginger and small frequent meals with vitamin B6 25 mg three times daily
options.rationale: Conservative dietary measures are appropriate for mild nausea, not for established hyperemesis with weight loss, hypokalaemia and ketonuria. (Source: RANZCOG Hyperemesis Gravidarum)

options.text: Outpatient subcutaneous ondansetron with home electrolyte and weight monitoring
options.rationale: Home subcutaneous therapy will not correct the established postural drop and hypokalaemia; admission allows monitored repletion. (Source: RANZCOG Hyperemesis Gravidarum)

options.text: Admit for intravenous thiamine 100 mg, ondansetron, crystalloid and potassium replacement
options.rationale: Severe hyperemesis with weight loss above 5 percent, ketonuria and electrolyte derangement requires admission; intravenous thiamine before dextrose-containing fluids prevents Wernicke encephalopathy. (Source: RANZCOG Hyperemesis Gravidarum)

options.text: Refer to obstetrics at the next available clinic with no immediate change to therapy
options.rationale: Delayed review fails to address acute dehydration, hypokalaemia and risk of Wernicke encephalopathy from depleted thiamine. (Source: RANZCOG Hyperemesis Gravidarum)

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum is severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy associated with weight loss above 5 percent of baseline, dehydration, electrolyte derangement and ketonuria. Australian admission criteria include any of these features or failure of outpatient therapy. Intravenous thiamine 100 mg is given before any dextrose-containing fluid to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. Ondansetron, metoclopramide and prochlorperazine are acceptable antiemetics; corticosteroids are reserved for refractory disease.
key_points: Weight loss over 5 percent or ketonuria warrants admission.
Give intravenous thiamine before dextrose-containing fluids.
Replace potassium when below 3.5 mmol/L on admission.
Corticosteroids are reserved for refractory hyperemesis.
explanation.pearls: Always check TSH and free T4 in severe hyperemesis; transient gestational thyrotoxicosis from beta-hCG cross-reactivity is common and self-limiting but missing primary thyrotoxicosis can be catastrophic in pregnancy.
